name: Classic Rosemary Focaccia

steps.instruction: Mix flour, yeast, and salt in a large bowl. Add warm water and 2 tablespoons olive oil.
Stir until a shaggy dough forms. Cover and let rest for 10 minutes.
With wet hands, fold the dough over itself 4 times, rotating the bowl. Cover and rest 30 minutes. Repeat this fold 2 more times.
Pour 2 tablespoons olive oil into a 9x13 inch baking pan. Transfer dough to pan, turn to coat in oil.
Cover and let rise until doubled and bubbly, about 1 hour.
Preheat oven to 220°C (425°F). Oil your fingers and press them into the dough to create dimples all over.
Drizzle generously with olive oil. Scatter rosemary and flaky salt over the top.
Bake until golden brown, 20-25 minutes. Let cool slightly before slicing.

tips: Use the best olive oil you have - you'll taste it
Don't skip the dimpling - it creates pockets for the oil
The dough should be very wet and sticky - that's correct
For extra flavor, infuse the oil with garlic before drizzling

name: Best Banana Bread

steps.instruction: Preheat oven to 175°C (350°F). Grease a 9x5 inch loaf pan.
Mash bananas in a large bowl until mostly smooth with some chunks remaining.
Stir in melted butter, then mix in brown sugar, egg, and vanilla.
Sprinkle flour, baking soda, and salt over the wet mixture. Fold gently until just combined - don't overmix!
Fold in walnuts if using. Pour batter into prepared pan.
Bake for 55-65 minutes until a toothpick comes out clean. The top should be golden and cracked.
Let cool in pan for 10 minutes, then turn out onto a wire rack.

tips: Freeze overripe bananas for later - they're perfect for this
A crack on top is normal and desirable
Add chocolate chips for a sweeter version
Wrapping in foil keeps it moist for days

name: Garlic Butter Naan

steps.instruction: Mix warm milk, sugar, and yeast. Let sit until foamy, about 5 minutes.
Combine flour, salt, and baking powder. Add yogurt, oil, and yeast mixture. Mix to form a soft dough.
Knead for 5 minutes until smooth and elastic. The dough should be soft but not sticky.
Cover and let rise in a warm place for 1 hour until doubled.
Mix melted butter with minced garlic. Set aside.
Divide dough into 8 pieces. Roll each into an oval about 5mm thick.
Heat a cast iron skillet over high heat. Cook naan for 1-2 minutes per side until puffed and charred in spots.
Brush hot naan with garlic butter and sprinkle with cilantro. Serve immediately.

tips: High heat is key - the pan should be almost smoking
Don't roll too thin or it won't puff
Cover cooked naan with a towel to keep warm
No cast iron? Use the highest broiler setting in your oven

name: Traditional Challah

steps.instruction: Dissolve yeast in warm water with a pinch of honey. Let bloom for 5 minutes.
Whisk together eggs, remaining honey, oil, and salt in a large bowl.
Add yeast mixture and half the flour. Stir until smooth. Gradually add remaining flour.
Knead for 10 minutes until smooth, elastic, and slightly tacky. The dough should pass the windowpane test.
Place in an oiled bowl, cover, and let rise until doubled, about 1.5 hours.
Punch down and divide into 3 equal pieces. Roll each into a 40cm rope.
Braid the three ropes together, pinching ends and tucking under. Place on a lined baking sheet.
Cover and let rise for 45 minutes until puffy.
Preheat oven to 175°C (350°F). Beat remaining egg and brush over challah. Sprinkle with sesame seeds.
Bake for 30-35 minutes until deep golden and hollow-sounding when tapped.

tips: For a shiny crust, apply two coats of egg wash
Braid loosely - the dough will expand
This bread freezes beautifully
Try a 6-strand braid for special occasions

name: Irish Soda Bread

steps.instruction: Preheat oven to 220°C (425°F). Line a baking sheet with parchment.
Whisk flour, baking soda, and salt in a large bowl.
Make a well in the center. Pour in buttermilk.
Using a fork, quickly mix from the center outward until a shaggy dough forms. Don't overwork!
Turn out onto a floured surface. Gently shape into a round about 5cm thick.
Place on baking sheet. Cut a deep cross on top, going almost to the edges.
Bake for 15 minutes at 220°C, then reduce to 200°C (400°F) and bake 25-30 more minutes.
The bread is done when it sounds hollow when tapped on the bottom. Cool on a wire rack.

tips: No buttermilk? Add 1 tbsp lemon juice to regular milk
Work quickly - the soda starts reacting immediately
Best eaten the day it's baked
Add raisins and caraway seeds for a sweeter version

name: Rustic Ciabatta

steps.instruction: Mix flour, yeast, and water until combined. Cover and rest for 20 minutes (autolyse).
Add salt and olive oil. Squeeze dough through your fingers to incorporate. The dough will be very wet.
Every 30 minutes for 2 hours, fold the dough: stretch one side up and fold over, rotate, repeat 4 times.
Cover and refrigerate overnight, or let rise at room temperature for 2 more hours.
Generously flour a work surface. Gently turn out dough, trying not to deflate it.
Divide in half. Gently stretch each piece into a rough rectangle. Don't worry about perfection!
Transfer to floured towels. Cover and proof for 45 minutes.
Preheat oven to 230°C (450°F) with a baking stone or inverted sheet pan.
Flip loaves onto hot stone. Bake 20-25 minutes until deeply golden.

tips: This dough is supposed to be wet and messy - embrace it
Handle the dough gently to preserve the air bubbles
A cold overnight rise develops more flavor
Spray oven with water at the start for a crispier crust

name: Classic French Brioche

steps.instruction: In a stand mixer with dough hook, combine flour, sugar, salt, and yeast.
Add eggs one at a time, mixing well after each. Add warm milk.
Knead on medium speed for 10 minutes until dough is smooth and pulls away from sides.
With mixer running, add butter one cube at a time. Knead until fully incorporated and dough is glossy.
Cover bowl and refrigerate for at least 2 hours or overnight. Cold dough is easier to shape.
Divide dough into 12 pieces. Roll each into a ball and place in greased muffin tin or brioche molds.
Cover and let rise at room temperature for 1 hour until doubled.
Preheat oven to 190°C (375°F). Brush tops with egg yolk mixed with 1 tbsp water.
Bake for 20-25 minutes until deep golden brown. Cool in pan for 5 minutes.

tips: Don't rush the butter - it must be fully incorporated
Cold dough = easier shaping, so don't skip the chill
The dough should be sticky but manageable
Makes incredible French toast the next day

name: French Baguette

steps.instruction: Mix flour, water, and yeast until just combined. Cover and rest 30 minutes (autolyse).
Add salt. Knead by hand or mixer for 8 minutes until smooth and elastic.
Place in oiled bowl. Every 30 minutes for 1.5 hours, fold dough onto itself from all sides.
Divide into 3 equal pieces. Pre-shape into loose logs. Rest 20 minutes.
Final shape: flatten each piece, fold in thirds, then roll to 35cm length with tapered ends.
Place on floured couche or towel, seam-side up. Cover and proof 45 minutes.
Preheat oven to 245°C (475°F) with a baking stone and steam pan.
Flip baguettes onto peel, score with 3-4 diagonal slashes. Add ice cubes to steam pan.
Bake with steam for 10 minutes, then remove steam pan. Bake 15 more minutes until dark golden.

tips: Steam in the first 10 minutes creates the crispy crust
Score at a 45-degree angle for proper 'ears'
Listen for the crackle when it comes out - that's a good sign
Eat within hours - these stale quickly but make great croutons

name: Sourdough Boule

steps.instruction: Mix flours and water. Cover and rest 30-60 minutes (autolyse).
Add starter and salt. Squeeze and fold to incorporate fully.
Every 30 minutes for 3 hours, perform stretch and folds (stretch dough up, fold over itself, rotate, repeat).
Cover and bulk ferment at room temperature 3-4 more hours until doubled and jiggly.
Gently turn dough onto floured surface. Pre-shape into a round. Rest 20 minutes.
Final shape: flip smooth-side down, fold edges to center, flip over and drag toward you to create tension.
Place seam-side up in rice-floured banneton. Cover and refrigerate 8-12 hours.
Preheat oven to 260°C (500°F) with Dutch oven inside for 45 minutes.
Turn dough into hot pot, score with razor, cover with lid. Bake 20 minutes.
Remove lid, reduce to 230°C (450°F). Bake 20-25 more minutes until deep mahogany.

tips: Your starter must be active and bubbly - do the float test
Temperature matters: 24-26°C is ideal for fermentation
The cold retard develops flavor - don't skip it
Let the bread cool completely before cutting (hardest part!)

name: Homemade Pita Bread

steps.instruction: Combine warm water, sugar, and yeast. Let sit 5 minutes until foamy.
Mix flours and salt. Add yeast mixture and olive oil. Stir to form a shaggy dough.
Knead for 8 minutes until smooth and elastic.
Place in oiled bowl, cover, and let rise for 1 hour until doubled.
Divide dough into 8 pieces. Roll each into a ball, cover, and rest 15 minutes.
Preheat oven to maximum (260°C/500°F) with baking stone or inverted sheet pan.
Roll each ball into a 15cm circle, about 3mm thick. Don't make them too thin!
Place 2-3 pitas directly on hot stone. Bake 2-3 minutes until puffed and lightly golden.
Stack baked pitas under a towel to keep them soft. Serve warm or at room temperature.

tips: Maximum oven heat is crucial for the puff
Roll evenly - thick spots won't puff properly
Don't open the oven door while baking
Store in plastic bags to keep soft for days

name: Cast Iron Cornbread

steps.instruction: Place 10-inch cast iron skillet in oven. Preheat to 220°C (425°F) for 15 minutes.
Whisk cornmeal, flour, baking powder, and salt in a large bowl.
In another bowl, whisk buttermilk, eggs, melted butter, and honey if using.
Pour wet ingredients into dry. Stir until just combined - lumps are okay.
Carefully remove hot skillet. Add 2 tbsp butter, swirl to coat bottom and sides.
Pour batter into sizzling butter. It should immediately start to set at the edges.
Bake 20-25 minutes until golden on top and a toothpick comes out clean.
Let cool 5 minutes in pan. Slice into wedges and serve with butter.

tips: The hot pan creates the crispy bottom crust - don't skip it
True Southern cornbread has no sugar - add honey if you prefer sweet
Bacon drippings instead of butter is traditional
Great with chili, collard greens, or just butter and honey

name: Soft Bavarian Pretzels

steps.instruction: Combine warm water, sugar, and yeast. Let sit 5 minutes until foamy.
Add flour, salt, and melted butter. Mix and knead for 8 minutes until smooth.
Cover and let rise in a warm place for 1 hour until doubled.
Divide dough into 8 pieces. Roll each into a 60cm rope.
Form pretzel shape: make a U, cross ends twice, fold down to bottom of U, press to attach.
Preheat oven to 230°C (450°F). Line baking sheets with parchment.
Bring 2L water to boil with baking soda (careful - it will foam!). Reduce to simmer.
Dip each pretzel in the soda bath for 30 seconds. Place on prepared sheets.
Sprinkle with coarse salt. Bake 12-15 minutes until deep brown.
Brush with melted butter while hot. Serve with mustard or cheese sauce.

tips: The baking soda bath gives pretzels their distinctive flavor and color
Traditional German pretzels use food-grade lye instead of baking soda
Don't skip the salt - it's essential to the pretzel experience
Best eaten warm on the same day
